top_requirements.requirement: Cloud platform fluency (AWS most common, then GCP/Azure)
top_requirements.approx_frequency: 389 of 586 mention AWS, 208 GCP, 174 Azure - roughly two-thirds touch at least one major cloud
100 of 586 (~17%), overlapping with 73 bug-bounty mentions, 52 red-team, 24 Burp Suite

top_requirements.requirement: Security certifications (CISSP most common, then OSCP/CISM/CEH)
top_requirements.approx_frequency: CISSP in 85 (~15%), OSCP 31, CISM 21, CEH 10 - common but usually preferred, not hard-gating
SAST-related terms in 189, DAST in 65, Semgrep named specifically in 21

top_requirements.requirement: Compliance framework fluency (NIST, SOC 2, ISO 27001, PCI, FedRAMP)
top_requirements.approx_frequency: NIST 175, ISO 27001 69, SOC 2 68, PCI 29, FedRAMP 22 - common as context, rarely the sole job focus

top_requirements.requirement: AI/LLM security exposure (threat modeling agentic systems, prompt injection)
top_requirements.approx_frequency: LLM in 123 of 586 (~21%), 'agentic' in 121, 'AI security' in 76, 'prompt injection' in 56 - now mainstream, not fringe
64 of 586 (~11%), concentrated almost entirely at defense/gov contractors (Anduril 28, Palantir 14, Boeing 8, GDIT 8)

top_requirements.requirement: 5+ years of relevant experience as modal bar
top_requirements.approx_frequency: "5 years" is the single most-cited experience threshold across postings that state one, consistent with senior-leaning hiring

top_responsibilities: Design and review security architecture/controls for new and existing systems, often via formal design-review or threat-modeling processes gating launches.
Run vulnerability and risk assessments across applications, cloud infrastructure, and networks, then track remediation to closure rather than just reporting findings.
Own or rotate through incident response: detection, triage, containment, and postmortem writing, frequently with a formal on-call rotation (on-call named in 79 of 586 postings).

Embed with product/engineering teams as a security partner - reviewing PRs and designs, writing secure-coding guidance, and being measured on whether developers actually adopt tooling/guardrails rather than just whether the guardrails exist ("cross-functional" appears in 183 postings, "embedded" in 116).
Build and maintain detection engineering and SIEM content (correlation rules, alerting logic) for a security operations function.
Integrate security scanning (SAST/DAST/dependency scanning) directly into CI/CD pipelines and developer tooling (DevSecOps), rather than as a separate late-stage gate.
Administer and harden IAM: least-privilege access reviews, SSO/identity provider integration, secrets management, and access-certification processes.

Increasingly, threat-model and red-team AI/agentic product surfaces specifically - prompt injection, tool/MCP-server misuse, and LLM-driven pipelines - as a named, growing slice of scope rather than a hypothetical.
Maintain and evidence compliance postures (SOC 2, ISO 27001, PCI, FedRAMP, NIST) as an engineering-adjacent responsibility, translating audit requirements into concrete controls.
At senior/staff/principal levels: run or scale a security program area (e.g., detection engineering, product security) and mentor other engineers, with less individual ticket-level work.

comp_signals.range_text: $256,800 - $321,000 (top band shown, Principal Security Engineer, Product & AI)
$135,000 - $200,000 (Application Security Engineer) vs. $90,000 - $150,000 for Forward Deployed Security Engineer - US Government - a notably lower band for the government-facing track

seniority_distribution_notes: Title-string analysis of all 586 postings: 210 explicitly "Senior", 105 "Staff", 23 "Principal", 12 "Lead" - so roughly 60% carry a senior-or-above title marker. Only 2 postings used junior/associate/entry language and none said "intern." The remaining ~226 titles had no seniority marker (plain "Security Engineer" or a numbered level like "II"), which in practice skew mid-level given the "5 years" modal experience bar found in text. Net: this is a senior-weighted archetype with a real staff/principal ceiling at scale, and almost no true entry-level postings in this live sample - consistent with the existing brief's claim that the "security engineer" title itself is reached only after a multi-year SOC-analyst or engineering on-ramp, not an entry point.
notable_divergence_from_existing_brief: Two findings worth flagging for review. (1) Comp: the brief leans on levels.fyi aggregator medians plus a single primary-source anchor (Anthropic's $320K-$485K). The live corpus actually contains ~150 postings with explicit stated dollar ranges (Lever/Greenhouse/Ashby postings routinely disclose pay bands) spanning roughly $90K (Palantir's government-facing Forward Deployed Security Engineer) up to $330K (CoreWeave Principal). This is a much richer primary-source comp dataset than the brief currently cites, and it also surfaces a pattern the brief misses entirely: postings tied to government/defense work (Palantir's "Forward Deployed Security Engineer - US Government" at $90K-$150K) pay meaningfully below the equivalent commercial-track roles at the same company ($135K-$200K), which the brief's "base-heavy, general SWE parity" framing doesn't capture. (2) AI/agentic security: the brief treats AI/frontier security as a single emerging example (Anthropic's Staff+ posting). In this sample, LLM/agentic-system security shows up as a mainstream, cross-company pattern - 123 of 586 postings (~21%) mention LLMs and 121 mention \"agentic\" systems specifically, well beyond frontier labs (fintechs, dev-tool companies, and infra companies all show it). The brief's framing of AI security as a narrow, name-one-company frontier niche likely undersells how broadly it's already baked into mainstream security-engineer scope as of mid-2026.

dimension_evidence.note: 223 of 586 postings explicitly reference threat modeling, and AI/agentic-system security work (121 postings) inherently means defining scope/risk for systems without established frameworks - consistent with target 4.

On-call/incident response appears in a meaningful minority (79 of 586 explicit on-call mentions, 240 incident-response mentions broadly) but the majority of listed responsibilities (architecture, IAM, compliance, CI/CD integration) read as plannable work - moderate target 3 looks right, not higher.

79 postings name on-call explicitly and 240 reference incident response as a responsibility; detection-and-response-titled roles (Roblox Principal Detection and Response Engineer, Vercel Security Engineer Detection Response, CoreWeave Advanced Response) are a recurring distinct sub-track, supporting a high but not universal (weight 0.7, not 1.0) target.

Vulnerability assessment plus incident postmortems plus multi-layer AI/cloud/app stacks in the current sample support a high diagnostic-depth target; nothing in the sample suggests it should be lower.

Architecture-review responsibilities are common (design reviews, IAM architecture) but framed around defense/control design rather than scaling throughput/load, matching the brief's 'architecture-for-defense, not scale' framing at a moderate target.

Postings cluster into named sub-tracks (AppSec, cloud security, detection & response, IAM, corporate/network security) confirming a deep-in-one-lane pattern rather than broad generalism - target 3 (moderate, not maximal) is reasonable.

Remediation tracking (129 vulnerability-management mentions) and developer-adoption framing (183 cross-functional, 116 embedded mentions) support accountability for outcomes beyond just producing a report.
183 postings mention cross-functional work and 116 mention embedded partnership with product/engineering teams, strongly corroborating the brief's 'embedded partner, not gatekeeper' framing.
Secure-coding education/consultation shows up as a responsibility pattern but is internal 1:few enablement, not a headline responsibility in most postings sampled - low-moderate target looks right.
No postings in the sample describe an external client/account-portfolio structure; work is organized around internal systems/teams, confirming target 1.
Same as above - no external relationship-continuity structure appears anywhere in the sample.

All ~150 stated comp figures in the sample are flat base salary ranges (occasionally plus equity/bonus language typical of standard SWE comp), with no sales-linked or utilization-linked variable component observed anywhere - confirms target 1.
Python appears in 352 of 586 postings (60%) and Go in ~72-112 depending on variant, plus SAST/Semgrep rule-writing (21 postings) - strong evidence for a real coding bar, supporting target 4.
No travel or physical-site-embed language found in a scan of the sample; target 1 holds.

223 of 586 postings (38%) explicitly reference threat modeling as a work product, plus 100 penetration-testing, 73 bug-bounty, and 52 red-team mentions - this is the strongest, most consistent signal in the whole corpus and squarely justifies the max target/weight.
No hardware/power/timing/RF constraint language found; domain is uniformly software/cloud/network even in defense-contractor postings (Anduril, Boeing) sampled - target 1 holds.
Staff/Principal-titled postings (105 + 23 = 128 of 586) commonly mention mentoring but rarely formal headcount/people-management ownership - low-moderate target is consistent with the sample.

No postings in the sample describe public speaking, content, or external-facing visibility as a responsibility (bug bounty programs are the closest analog but are process ownership, not public-facing content work) - target 1 holds.

Given that 123 of 586 postings (21%) now reference LLMs and 121 reference agentic systems, ML/AI-system fluency is showing up far more broadly than a single named frontier-lab exception - worth reviewing whether target/weight should move up modestly for the archetype as a whole, even though most postings still don't require hands-on ML engineering (they require securing AI systems, not building them, which is a real distinction worth preserving).
No meaningful mobile-specific security requirements surfaced in the keyword/text scan; target 1 holds.
No data-pipeline/data-engineering-specific requirements found beyond generic cloud infra; target 1 holds.

Cloud terms are extremely pervasive in this sample - AWS 389/586 (66%), GCP 208, Azure 174, Kubernetes 189, Terraform 124 - which is a notably higher hit rate than a target-2/weight-0.2 (low-moderate) treatment implies, even accounting for the brief's rationale that adversarial_threat_modeling is the 'real' gate. Worth a reviewer double-check on whether cloud-infrastructure fluency is underweighted relative to how often postings actually require it explicitly, separate from the threat-modeling axis.
